expectations.text: Build handoff names Claude-Code-specific capture mechanisms (hooks, MCP servers)
expectations.evidence: build-handoff.md line 11: 'Use hooks (`PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse`, `Stop`), MCP servers, structured tool result parsing for telemetry.' Spec also names same hooks at line 480.

expectations.text: Session notes describe what happened in each of the 8 phases
expectations.evidence: session-notes.md contains explicit headings 'Phase 1 - Working Backwards', 'Phase 2 - Metrics Design', 'Phase 3 - Apply the Algorithm', 'Phase 4 - Probe for Gaps (sequential fallback)', 'Phase 5 - Verification Suite (TDD anchor)', 'Phase 6 - Draft', 'Phase 7 - Verify Adversarially (qa-agents simulated)', 'Phase 8 - Build Handoff and Final Assertions'. Each section has substantive content (gate outcomes, soft fails, findings).

findings.what: qa-agents pass was simulated in-context by a single agent role-playing Finder/Auditor/Referee. The skill's Phase 7 is explicitly designed to use three independent agents to catch context-bleed and confirmation bias. Self-simulated adversarial review provides materially weaker assurance than independent agents, yet the spec was still promoted to status: verified on that basis.
findings.why_it_matters: A real Finder run with an independent context might have surfaced findings the simulated Finder rationalized away. The status: verified frontmatter signals 'this passed adversarial review' to downstream consumers (build agent, automation), but the assurance behind that label is weaker than the contract implies. A2 user trusting the verified label may underestimate risk.

findings.where: Phase 4 MECE fan-out (A8) + Procedure step coverage
findings.what: Phase 4's four MECE sub-agents ran sequentially in one context rather than as parallel independent agents. Sequential single-context probing is biased by earlier findings (anchoring) and cannot catch the same blind spots that four cold-start agents would. The spec acknowledges this in A8 but does not flag any specific finding category as therefore at risk.
findings.why_it_matters: Categories likely under-covered: 'Agent C - failure modes' typically benefits most from independent perspectives. The spec's failure-mode coverage may be biased toward failure modes Mike already mentioned (Greg ping-pong, figure drift) and miss orthogonal ones (e.g. agent prompt-injection from the milestones log, model-IP leakage in figure-provenance appendix).

findings.what: Classification depends on natural-language parsing of Greg's reply ('looks good', 'approved', 'ship it'). No threshold is set for confidence, no human fallback is named for ambiguous replies that aren't 'approved with one tweak'. E.g. 'Mostly fine, but the runway figure feels off - can you double-check?' - is that a factual correction or a narrative edit? The spec's edge case 'corrections >0 vs edits >2' assumes the classifier reliably distinguishes the two.
findings.why_it_matters: If the classifier mislabels a hedged factual question as a narrative edit, the agent runs revise_narrative (no figure re-check), the figure stays stale, and the update ships wrong. This is precisely the bug class the spec exists to fix - figure-lock-before-draft solves the timing of figure pulls but does not solve classification fragility.

findings.where: Edge Cases - 'Financial model not closed for quarter' AND Preconditions
findings.what: Preconditions states 'The financial model has been closed for the quarter (book-close complete)'. Edge Cases handles 'not closed' via abort_book_not_closed. But there is no detection mechanism for a partially-closed model (some cells closed, others not). The kickoff Decision Rule looks for a 'BOOK_CLOSED_<quarter>' cell or Mike's word, which is binary - it doesn't detect the realistic case where book-close is in progress and some KPI cells are stable while others aren't.
findings.why_it_matters: Mike says 'yes book is closed' on Monday because revenue is closed; cash isn't reconciled until Wednesday. lock_figures runs Monday, snapshots cash from a not-yet-closed reconciliation, lock_figures stability check passes (cell hasn't been edited in 48h because nobody's touching it), figures ship wrong. The 48h-since-last-edit heuristic is a stability proxy, not a closure proxy.

findings.where: Output - figure-provenance appendix + Build Notes Out-of-scope
findings.what: Output spec says appendix contains 'cell references back to the financial model' delivered as an attachment. No redaction or scoping rule is named for the appendix. The financial model likely contains figures, formulas, and structure that GIX investors should NOT see (e.g. forward forecasts, uncited operating costs, employee comp, other LP details). 'Cell URL + value + as-of date' could leak sheet structure or per-cell formulas if the appendix format isn't tightly constrained.
findings.why_it_matters: An investor clicks the Drive cell-URL link in the appendix, lands in a sheet they're not authorized to see (Drive ACL works in their favor, but sheet layout is exposed in screenshots). Or the appendix template inadvertently includes a formula text. Information disclosure to LPs of competitively sensitive data is a real failure mode for an investor-update process.

findings.where: A10 (Open question) - securities-law / forward-looking statements review
findings.what: Open Question A10 acknowledges there's no compliance/legal review gate for forward-looking statements, performance claims, or securities-law language. This is flagged as 'Decision needed before first production run' - meaning the spec was promoted to status: verified with a known compliance gap that could materially affect production use.
findings.why_it_matters: An agent-generated forward-look section could include statements that, in a private placement or LP context, trigger Reg D safe-harbor issues or implied performance guarantees. Mike sends, content is bad, GIX or counsel surfaces it months later. A spec with status: verified that flags this as 'TBD before production' is contradictory - either the compliance gate is in scope (then add it) or the spec is verified-for-design-only (then status should reflect that).

findings.where: Procedure step 11 greg_review SLA timer + Build Notes
findings.what: SLA is '3 business days', Build Notes specifies 'wall-clock business-day timer (skip weekends and US federal holidays)'. No definition of which holiday calendar (US federal vs. NY exchange vs. company-specific). Greg may live in a different timezone or country - 'business day' isn't defined relative to whose calendar.
findings.why_it_matters: Edge case where Greg is traveling and a US-only holiday extends an effective SLA, or Greg's local Friday holiday is a US business day and the timer expires unfairly. Low impact because Mike-override is named, but worth pinning.

findings.where: Verification Suite - 'Highlight selection is correctly attributed to Mike'
findings.what: This semantic check is classified as agent-verified by inspection. The check name is concrete now (post-fix) but the verification method - 'agent reads spec and confirms' - is exactly the kind of self-judging verification that the qa-agents pattern exists to harden. There's no external script or test that fails if a future spec edit silently moves highlight-selection ownership to the agent.
findings.why_it_matters: Spec rot: a future maintainer 'optimizes' select_highlights to be agent-decided to save a Mike interruption, the semantic check still passes on agent self-inspection, the highlight-Mike-judgment intent is lost. Low because Mike will likely notice the first run, but a structural assertion (e.g. 'Requirement Owners table for select_highlights row contains the literal string Mike') would be stronger.

findings.what: Parallelization section says gather_milestones, load_prior_update, apply_prior_feedback can run in parallel after lock_figures. The Mermaid diagram and Procedure successors render them as a strict sequential chain S4 -> S5 -> S6. The diagram doesn't reflect the stated parallelism. Build agent reads the diagram first, implements sequential, parallelization opportunity is lost.
findings.why_it_matters: Minor cycle-time optimization missed; spec is internally inconsistent (diagram and Parallelization section disagree). Build Notes does say 'build agent decides' but the diagram is more directly authoritative for most implementers.

findings.what: Verification Record claims 'Auditor disprovals: 3 (F1, F3, F6)' and 'Referee net: 3 confirmed real findings (F2 metric gap, F4 drafting, F5 edge-case gap)'. The arithmetic in session-notes adds up, but the Referee's role per qa-agents is to break Finder/Auditor disagreements - if Auditor disproved F1, F3, F6 cleanly, did Finder accept the disprovals or did Referee adjudicate? The record reads as Auditor's word being final, which conflates Auditor and Referee in the simulation.
findings.why_it_matters: Audit-trail clarity. If a future reviewer wants to second-guess whether F3 (time_to_investors_days threshold) was really disproved, the record doesn't show whether Finder agreed with the disproof or Referee was needed. Low impact, but a real qa-agents three-agent run would have an explicit Finder-rebuts-Auditor exchange or an Auditor-uncontested note.
